questions.questionText: End-tidal CO2 monitoring (capnography) waveform suddenly drops to zero during surgery. The most likely cause is:
questions.optionB: Esophageal intubation or circuit disconnection (no CO2 from lungs → flat capnography trace)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Sudden ETCO2 to zero = no gas exchange reaching monitor = disconnection or esophageal intubation. Why correct: Zero ETCO2 = no CO2 in expired gas. Causes: circuit disconnection (most common in OR), accidental extubation, esophageal intubation, complete airway obstruction, cardiac arrest. Why A wrong: Bronchospasm → elevated ETCO2 with 'shark-fin' waveform (prolonged expiratory upstroke), not zero. Why C wrong: PE → sudden DECREASE in ETCO2 (dead space increase) but not to absolute zero. Why D wrong: Hyperventilation → low but not zero ETCO2. Clinical relevance: Capnography: the most important monitor after pulse oximetry. Normal ETCO2 = 35-45 mmHg. Shape matters: shark-fin = bronchospasm. Rebreathing = elevated baseline. Exam trap: Sudden zero ETCO2 = check connections first (disconnect/misplacement). Gradual decrease = decreased cardiac output/PE. Memory aid: 'Zero CO2 = Zero connection (check tube and circuit).' Reference: Aitkenhead – Monitoring.

questions.questionText: Train-of-four (TOF) monitoring is used to assess:
questions.optionB: Degree of neuromuscular blockade (4 supramaximal stimuli at 2 Hz — counting fading twitches indicates level of NMB)
questions.explanation: Core concept: TOF = 4 stimuli at 2Hz → counting responses assesses NMB depth. Why correct: Non-depolarizing NMB: progressive fade (twitch 4 lost first → 3 → 2 → 1). TOF ratio = T4/T1. TOF ratio >0.9 = adequate recovery (safe to extubate). 0 twitches = deep block. 1 twitch = 90-95% block. 4 twitches with fade = partial block. Why A wrong: BIS (bispectral index) monitors depth of anesthesia (EEG-based). Why C wrong: Pain assessment = NRS/VAS scores. Why D wrong: BP = NIBP/arterial line. Clinical relevance: Adductor pollicis (ulnar nerve) is standard site. TOF ratio <0.9 at extubation → residual paralysis → respiratory complications. Reverse with neostigmine+glycopyrrolate or sugammadex (for rocuronium). Exam trap: Depolarizing block (succinylcholine) → NO fade (uniform decrease). Non-depolarizing → FADE. This differentiates block type. Memory aid: 'TOF = Tests Of Four twitches = quantifies neuromuscular Function.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Monitoring.

questions.questionText: BIS (Bispectral Index) monitoring during general anesthesia — the target range to prevent intraoperative awareness while avoiding excessive depth is:
questions.optionB: 40-60 (adequate surgical anesthesia — lower values = deeper anesthesia, higher values = more awake)
questions.explanation: Core concept: BIS monitors consciousness via processed EEG — target 40-60 during surgery. Why correct: BIS scale 0-100. 100 = fully awake. 40-60 = adequate general anesthesia (optimal range). <40 = deep anesthesia (risk of hypotension, prolonged recovery). >60 = light (risk of awareness). Why A wrong: 80-100 = awake/sedated state. Why C wrong: 20-30 = excessively deep. Why D wrong: 0 = burst suppression/isoelectric EEG. Clinical relevance: NAP5 (UK): intraoperative awareness incidence ~1:19,000. Risk factors: TIVA without BIS, cardiac/obstetric surgery, difficult airway scenarios. Exam trap: BIS is processed EEG, NOT raw EEG. It's most useful during TIVA (no MAC to judge depth). Not validated in children <1 year. Memory aid: 'BIS 40-60 = Balance between awareness and excessive depth.' Reference: Aitkenhead – Monitoring.
